one_line_takeaway: Across five SOTA hospital time-series generators and nine membership-inference attacks on MIMIC-IV/eICU data, models trained on more than ~500 patient records show no measurable membership-inference vulnerability (AUROC ~0.5) even without differential privacy, with overfitting -- not dimensionality -- as the key driver of leakage in smaller-sample regimes.
key_finding: All nine MIAs, whether or not they use auxiliary real data, fail to distinguish training members above chance level (AUROC ~0.50) once the generator's training set exceeds 500 samples, for both time-series and static-attribute generation, across all five models. Below 500 samples, vulnerability diverges by attack and model: Diffusion-TS is markedly more susceptible in low-sample regimes (e.g., AUROC up to 1.00 at Ntrain=25 for MC/DOMIAS-eq1/GAN-Leak-DTW attacks), while GAN-Leak-Breugel and DOMIAS-BNAF break down (fall to chance) already around Ntrain=100. Sanity checks confirm that when attacks are run directly on real training data (not synthetic proxies), most attacks succeed with above-chance performance except DOMIAS-BNAF and LOGAN-pb, which fail even in this best-case scenario for sample sizes above 250 -- indicating those two attacks are intrinsically weak for this data modality rather than the generators being private. The externally sourced eICU dataset works essentially as well as an in-distribution MIMIC-IV holdout as an auxiliary reference for the attacks, showing that cross-hospital data (not just leaked same-hospital data) suffices to mount effective membership inference when generators are undertrained. NRMSEmin (nearest-neighbor overfitting proxy) tracks attack success closely: Diffusion-TS shows the largest NRMSEmin (up to 0.097 at Ntrain=25), correlating with its higher MIA vulnerability, and overfitting -- not dimensionality -- is identified as the primary driver of privacy leakage, since low-dimensional static attributes show the same vulnerability pattern as high-dimensional time series.
core_contribution: The paper conducts the first systematic empirical privacy audit of state-of-the-art hospital time-series generative models (GHOSTS, GHOSTS-POST, HALO, KoVAE, Diffusion-TS) using a battery of nine established black-box and partial black-box membership inference attacks (MC/Monte Carlo, DOMIAS-eq1, DOMIAS-KDE, DOMIAS-BNAF, GAN-Leak-Breugel, GAN-Leak-DTW, GAN-Leak-cal, GAN-Leak-||.||, LOGAN-pb), plus an overfitting proxy metric (NRMSEmin). It provides (1) a taxonomy contextualizing existing privacy attacks for hospital time series, (2) an open-source reproducible framework for auditing arbitrary synthetic time-series generators, and (3) an empirical finding that MIA success against synthetic clinical data depends primarily on the training-set size of the generator, not on data dimensionality.
threat_model: The attacker's goal is membership inference: determine whether a specific patient's ICU record was part of the training set of a generative model, given access only to the model's synthetic output (black-box) or synthetic output plus an auxiliary/reference real dataset (partial black-box, e.g., a holdout split of the same hospital's data or an external hospital dataset such as eICU). White-box attacks (requiring model parameters/discriminator access) are explicitly excluded as unrealistic and easily avoided by not publishing the model. The paper treats the released synthetic dataset itself as the asset an adversary can obtain (e.g., through public release for research), and tests whether that release alone, or combined with auxiliary real records, leaks membership status.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) generators were trained on only a single dataset (MIMIC-IV), so the ~500-sample privacy threshold may not generalize to other datasets/cohorts and 'should not be considered universal'; (2) empirically measured attack success only provides a lower bound on the true probability of identity disclosure, not a formal upper-bound privacy guarantee; (3) the evaluation kept time-series and static attributes separate rather than combining longitudinal and demographic/clinical covariates jointly, so thresholds may shift for richer combined feature spaces; (4) the relationship between the number of retained PCA components (40, fixed) and the resulting privacy-utility trade-off was not quantified; (5) the study excludes attribute disclosure and model inversion attacks, reasoning (without independent verification) that MIA robustness implies attribute-disclosure robustness; (6) DOMIAS-BNAF's failure to replicate published results is attributed to BNAF's inability to estimate density for this data type, an unresolved implementation/method mismatch rather than a settled negative result about privacy.

datasets_used: MIMIC-IV (Johnson et al., 2023): 21,057 training + 1,282 validation ICU stays from 16,320/1,000 patients, 22,463 holdout stays from 17,321 patients; 9 clinical time-series variables over 288 time points plus static attributes (sex, age, marital status, length of stay, race)

eICU (Pollard et al., 2018): 8,348 ICU stays from 8,163 patients, used as external auxiliary/reference dataset for cross-hospital attack transferability (demographic attributes not extracted due to missing data)
quantitative_results: All 9 MIAs fall to chance-level AUROC (~0.50) for all 5 generators once generator training set size exceeds 500 samples, both for time series and static attributes
Diffusion-TS: highest vulnerability at small training sizes, e.g. AUROC up to 1.00 (MC, DOMIAS-eq1, GAN-Leak-DTW attacks) at Ntrain=25, dropping toward chance by Ntrain=250-500
GAN-Leak-Breugel and DOMIAS-BNAF attacks break down to chance level already around Ntrain=100, earlier than other attacks
NRMSEmin(Dsyn) overfitting proxy for Diffusion-TS reaches 0.097 at Ntrain=25, decreasing toward ~0.001-0.003 by Ntrain>=500, correlating with MIA success
Sanity checks (attacks run on real training data directly): most attacks succeed above chance, but DOMIAS-BNAF and LOGAN-pb fail even in this baseline for training sizes above 250

eICU-based partial black-box attacks (external hospital as auxiliary data) achieve AUROC performance comparable to attacks using a MIMIC-IV holdout partition, e.g. Diffusion-TS and KoVAE show high vulnerability under DOMIAS-KDE and GAN-Leak-cal attacks using either MIMIC-IV or eICU auxiliary data
Total experiment runtime: ~7 days on 128-core/256GB workstation with one NVIDIA A100 (40GB)
baselines_compared: GHOSTS (Zhumagambetov et al., 2024) - GAN-based hospital time-series generator with structured sparsity penalties
GHOSTS-POST - GHOSTS variant with additional postprocessing for realism
HALO (Theodorou et al., 2023) - hierarchical autoregressive transformer/masked-linear language model for EHR generation
KoVAE (Naiman et al., 2024) - Koopman-theory-inspired Variational Autoencoder for time series
Diffusion-TS (Yuan and Qiao, 2024) - diffusion-based multivariate time-series generator with trend/seasonal decomposition

Nine MIA methods compared against each other as attack baselines: MCAttack (Hilprecht et al., 2019), GAN-Leak-Chen/-Breugel/-cal/-DTW/-||.|| (Chen et al., 2020a/b; van Breugel et al., 2023), DOMIAS-eq1/-KDE/-BNAF (van Breugel et al., 2023), LOGAN-pb (Hayes et al., 2018, partial black-box variant only; the white-box LOGAN-wb variant was excluded as unrealistic)
